narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid LSAT score of 38—above the typical 36.7 average—your law school application stood out in some ways, but the admissions model ultimately predicted a 58.9% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, tipping the decision toward rejection. This prediction draws from patterns in data on thousands of students' academic paths, family backgrounds, and bar outcomes, focusing on who succeeds right out of the gate. The biggest factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, well below the average of about 5.5, signaling to the model that your later undergraduate performance placed you in the lowest group compared to peers, which strongly correlates with bar struggles. Next in influence was your LSAT, which actually worked in your favor by pushing the odds toward passing, thanks to that stronger-than-average score showing your potential for legal reasoning. Rounding out the top influences, your Year 1 decile of 3—lower than the typical 5.7—added some downward pressure, suggesting an early academic pattern that the model links to higher failure risk. Other details, like your lower-middle family income bracket and full-time student status (shared by over 90% of applicants), played smaller roles but didn't offset the key concerns. Compared to typical students who pass the bar first time, your later grades lag significantly behind their mid-range deciles, even if your LSAT gives you an edge over the average. It's tough to hear, especially when you've put in the effort to score well on the LSAT, but the model prioritizes consistent academic deciles as the strongest predictors of bar success. To move forward, consider retaking undergraduate courses or a post-bac program to boost your decile rankings, as even small improvements there could shift future predictions dramatically. You might also seek targeted bar prep resources early, leveraging that LSAT strength to build exam confidence. Admissions teams value persistence, so reapplying after addressing these gaps could open doors—many students in similar spots have turned it around. Keep focusing on what you control, and you'll have a stronger case next time.
